Date: Sun, 21 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: MAP Onboarding — QuantifyAI Private Deployment: Network/VPN + Key Owners

Josh — thanks again for the productive demo last week. As discussed, we’re kicking off a short Mutual Action Plan (MAP) to move QuantifyAI from POC to a production private deployment (VPC-connected) on Redwood Private. The goal is a go-live window in Q2; this MAP focuses first on network, VPN, and keys so we can validate connectivity and security controls early.

I’ve attached an initial MAP spreadsheet (MAP-QuantifyAI-draft-v1.xlsx) and a quick topology sketch. High-level requests from our side:

- Confirm your networking owner(s) and VPN point of contact (CIDR ranges, preferred VPN type: IPsec/Transit).
- Confirm IAM/KMS owner(s) and any specific key management policy (HSM/KMS provider, rotation cadence).
- Provide an internal stakeholder roster (names + roles + emails) for technical and procurement coordination.
- Please review the attached draft MAP timeline and mark any hard constraints (e.g., procurement windows, security review dates).

Proposed immediate next steps:
1) Technical kickoff (60 min) week of Mar 22 — attendees: Redwood infra + Quantify infra/security owners.
2) Exchange initial VPN config and test accounts within 5 business days after the kickoff.
3) Security artifacts checklist (SOC2 evidence, data retention, audit logs) — we will start gathering items in parallel.

Can you confirm the right contacts on your side and whether Mar 23 or Mar 24 morning PT works for the technical kickoff?

Thanks — looking forward to partnering on this.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments:
- MAP-QuantifyAI-draft-v1.xlsx (spreadsheet)

title: MAP: Dedicated & Private sizing — weekly signal review
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.com>
To: Josh Brunner <josh.brunner@nimbusai.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: MAP: Dedicated & Private sizing — weekly signal review

Josh / team —

Quick weekly update from our side on the MAP sizing signal for the Dedicated + Private deployment options. Attached is the working MAP spreadsheet (MAP-sizing-v3.xlsx) with the latest asks from Nimbus and our preliminary engineering guidance. High-level:

- Evaluation timeline: Nimbus evaluation kicks off next week (03/28) with a 3-week throughput test window.
- Toggle: they want a Dedicated SKU for US production and Private (VPC) for EU compliance.
- Primary asks from Nimbus: target steady-state throughput ~250 qps (short-text gen), p95 latency <120ms, and regional isolation.

What we need to lock this week:
1) GPU mix by region (type + count) for initial commit
2) Throughput targets per region and expected burst rate
3) Any hard constraints from procurement (pricing/lead times) — ProcureCo is CC'd on procurement questions.

Please review the GPU suggestions I added in the sheet (column "engineer_proposal"). I marked two options (cost-optimized vs latency-optimized) — we should pick one and confirm owners.

Next steps proposed:
- Kimberly: confirm EU H100 availability and tell us lead time
- Vivek: sizing validation + autoscale policy recommendation
- Laura: commercial delta for Dedicated vs Private pricing (if any)

Attaching: MAP-sizing-v3.xlsx (spreadsheet)

Thanks — Mateo
Mateo Alvarez
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
mateo@redwood.com




From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Josh Brunner <josh.brunner@nimbusai.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Mar 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP: Dedicated & Private sizing — weekly signal review

Thanks for the kickoff, Mateo. Quick corrections / clarifications after talking to procurement and capacity planning:

- EU region: H100 availability is constrained for April. We can commit to H100 (4x) for Private/EU but with a 3-week lead time for long-term reservations. Alternative: A100s (8x) provide similar peak throughput for the first 6 weeks at slightly higher operational overhead.
- US region: Recommend A100s for Dedicated initially (6x), with a plan to phase to H100 if Nimbus needs lower p95.

Updated throughput targets (proposal):
- EU (Private): steady 120 qps, burst 220 qps (short-text gen), p95 target <140ms
- US (Dedicated): steady 130 qps, burst 300 qps, p95 target <110ms

Action items I can take this week:
- Confirm exact lead times and reservation windows with hardware suppliers (ProcureCo).
- Update the MAP spreadsheet with a fallback plan (A100 fallback) and a note on expected token-cost delta.

Matt/engineering: please call out any kernel/quantization restrictions if we shift between A100 & H100; I flagged that in the sheet.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Head of Partnerships | Redwood Inference
kimberly@redwood.com



From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.com>, Josh Brunner <josh.brunner@nimbusai.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Mar 2027 11:26:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP: Dedicated & Private sizing — weekly signal review

Map sizing validation notes below (I validated the numbers in MAP-sizing-v3.xlsx and attached a short estimate):

- Proposed hardware (initial commit):
  * US - Dedicated: 6x A100-80GB (NVIDIA A100 80GB) || target steady throughput 130 qps, burst 300 qps.
  * EU - Private (VPC): 4x H100-80GB (NVIDIA H100 80GB) || target steady throughput 120 qps, burst 220 qps.

- Rationale: US workload has slightly higher burst tolerance so we scale with A100s where queued batching is more cost-effective. EU requires lower tail latency & compliance (VPC) so H100s are recommended for lower p95 in generation-heavy tests.

- Autoscaling/operational notes:
  * Configure continuous batching with max batch latency 15ms and a dynamic batch size capped per-GPU to keep p95 constraints.
  * KV cache pre-warming for evaluation runs to avoid cold-start p95 spikes.
  * Suggest canary: run first 72 hours on a 50% commit (3 A100s / 2 H100s) to validate throughput before full cutover.

- Risk items:
  * H100 procurement lead time (Kimberly to confirm)
  * Network egress during EU private testing — Laura, please confirm if this impacts contract terms.

title: Nimbus Health — Dedicated performance & reserved throughput validation
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Carter <emily.carter@nimbushealth.com>, Devon Santos <devon.santos@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 12 Mar 2027 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: Nimbus Health — post-call recap & proposed validation plan

Hi Emily / Devon —

Thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed validation plan to align on SLOs and dedicated sizing for your streaming use case:

Summary (what we heard)
- Primary SLO: 95th-percentile (p95) end-to-end latency <= 180ms for single-turn streaming completions for clinical note generation (average prompt length ~550 tokens).
- Target sustained throughput: 220 qps during daily peak window (09:00–11:00 PT).
- Concurrency pattern: bursts up to 550 concurrent sessions for short bursts (~10–15 minutes) during batch hours.
- Output streaming is required (progressive tokens) and KV cache reuse expected between follow-up requests.

Proposed validation plan (next 2 weeks)
1) Baseline bench: Redwood to run a controlled pilot on Dedicated test cluster using your sample workload (we'll need a representative prompt set + token length distribution).
2) Throughput sweep: increment qps from 50 -> 250 to identify sustained throughput ceiling and latency degradation points.
3) Burst/ concurrency tests: synthetic spike tests to validate 550 concurrent session behavior for short windows.
4) GPU matrix: test configs on (A) A100-80GB float16, (B) H100-80GB, (C) 8x4090-equivalent pooled inference nodes — produce cost/latency tradeoffs.
5) Streaming fidelity: ensure progressive token delivery meets p95 SLO without large tail stalls; report token-by-token latencies.

Deliverables and timing
- Day 0: You share sample prompts + throughput profile (see items requested below).
- Day 2–5: Redwood runs baseline and initial throughput sweep; we’ll share interim dashboard and SLO curves.
- Day 6–10: Burst tests + GPU matrix results + recommended reserved throughput and config for Dedicated.
- Day 11: Joint review call to sign off on sizing.

Action items for Nimbus
- Share a sample prompt set (20–50 prompts representative of live traffic) and a token-length histogram.
- Confirm acceptable p95 latency target (you mentioned 180ms; flag if that needs to be tighter).
- Provide a list of any non-negotiable model features (e.g., tool-calling hooks, function outputs) that could affect kernel selection.

Attachments: slo-sizing-matrix.xlsx (baseline fields + what we'll fill), gpu-comparison-onepager.pdf

If this looks good I'll schedule the pilot run and loop in Rishi to own execution.

Thanks,
Mateo
Sr. Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
[attachment: slo-sizing-matrix.xlsx] [attachment: gpu-comparison-onepager.pdf]

-----Original Message-----
From: Emily Carter <emily.carter@nimbushealth.com>
Sent: Fri, Mar 12, 2027 at 13:05
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
Subject: Re: Discovery call — performance needs

Thanks Mateo — this captures the call well. A couple clarifications from our side:
- The 180ms is our aspirational p95 for internal tooling; for external patient-facing completions we may accept up to 250ms.
- Expected average tokens per completion: 420 tokens. Peak token-heavy workflows (batch note expansion) can hit 1,200 tokens but are infrequent (~2% of traffic).
- We can share a sample set by EOD Monday.

Can Redwood include the explicit cost delta between A100 and H100 in the GPU matrix? We need rough ops cost per million tokens as part of our internal procurement review.

Emily
Director of Engineering, Nimbus Health

-----Reply (Mateo)-----
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Carter <emily.carter@nimbushealth.com>, Devon Santos <devon.santos@nimbushealth.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Mar 2027 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Nimbus Health — post-call recap & proposed validation plan

Emily — great, thanks for the clarifications. Updated plan notes:
- We'll treat 180ms as the internal baseline and include a second curve for 250ms external-facing allowance.
- We'll make sure the GPU matrix includes per-million-token cost estimates (run-cost only) and an ops-friendly summary table.

Please upload the prompt set and token histogram to the Drive link below (or reply with a zipped sample). Also, please tell us if you expect model-serving to need private VPC (we can run pilot in Dedicated test cluster or in a private sandbox depending on compliance).

Drive: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1aB2cD3_SLO-Validation

I’ll schedule the baseline run for Tue/Wed and loop Rishi to run the GPU matrix.

Mateo

-----Internal: Vanessa's note to thread (internal-only)-----
From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 16 Mar 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Nimbus Health validation planning (internal)

Quick internal summary / recommendation:
- For streaming p95 180ms at 220 qps with bursts to 550 concurrent, H100 gives best headroom; A100 likely OK at lower concurrency with slight latency tail risk.
- Cost tradeoff: H100 ~20–30% more expensive but reduces required reserved throughput by ~25% on our estimates; include a table showing reserved qps vs instance count.
- Recommend we propose two options to Nimbus: (1) H100 conservative config for SLA-critical workloads; (2) A100 cost-optimized config with autoscale headroom and a 250ms tier.

I’ll prepare the GPU matrix PDF (attached earlier) and a short note for procurement on contract sizing.

-Vanessa

-----Internal: Rishi's follow-up (final)-----
From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwood.ai>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 16 Mar 2027 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Nimbus Health validation planning (internal)

I can run the GPU matrix and the throughput sweep on our Dedicated test cluster this week. I'll use the validation-script.sh (attached) to standardize runs and produce per-token latency histograms + p95 curves.

Deliverables I'll drop in Drive:
- raw-benchmarks.csv
- p95-latency-curve.png
- recommended-reserved-throughput.md

Linking HubSpot deal for records: https://hubspot.com/deal/NIM-2027-0421

I'll start once prompts are uploaded.

-Rishi
[attachment: validation-script.sh]

title: Performance hypothesis & probe plan for POC — NexaHealth
preview:
From: Sophie Lambert <sophie.lambert@nexahealth.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo_alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 8 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Intro / Discovery call notes — NexaHealth inference needs

Matt (and team) —

Thanks again for the demo this morning. Quick recap from our side and the things we agreed to surface:

- Primary workload: clinical summarization + triage suggestions for nurse inboxes. Payloads are relatively small (single-note summaries) but we have occasional multi-note aggregations.
- Expected concurrent users: ~120 endpoint clients during business hours, spiking up to ~700 for 10–20s when we run batch catch-ups at 02:00 local.
- Approx tokens per request: 250–1,200 (median ~430); but a small fraction (~5%) of requests hit ~2,400 tokens.
- P95 latency target from our app: 350ms (we know this is tight) for the 90% of requests under 800 tokens; batch/asynchronous flows can tolerate higher latency.
- Security: will need SOC2 + VPC peering for the POC; we can share the security questionnaire early next week.

Attachments: I've uploaded two artifacts to the Drive link below — a sample subset of payloads and our SLA draft. Please confirm the traffic profile assumptions and proposed next steps.

Sophie
Sophie Lambert
Head of Product Ops, NexaHealth
sophie.lambert@nexahealth.com

---

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1Nexa/POC-eval-matrix


From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo_alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Lambert <sophie.lambert@nexahealth.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 8 Mar 2027 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Performance hypothesis & probe plan for POC — NexaHealth

Thanks Sophie — solid context and appreciate the artifacts. Recap + proposed plan below (high level) so we all have the same starting point. I pulled Grace and Dev in because they will own runtime sizing and integration work.

Summary of what we heard (confirm or correct):
- Workload: clinical summarization + triage; mostly short requests, 5% long-tail (~2.4k tokens).
- Concurrency: 120 steady, burst up to 700 for 10–20s windows during batch jobs.
- Desired p95 latency: 350ms for requests <800 tokens.
- Security: SOC2 + VPC peering required for the POC.

Proposed POC probe plan (3-week technical POC, extendable):
1) Week 0 (this week): intake + sample payload validation — we confirm tokenization and representative QPS pattern.
2) Week 1: controlled load probes — run staged RPS ramp to characterize latency curve (p50/p95/p99) across short, medium, long token bins (<=400, 401–1200, 1201–2400). We will include KV cache warm/cold runs.
3) Week 2: burst/soak and integration — simulate business-hour concurrency plus night batch spikes; exercise fallback routing to smaller model if SLOs violated.
4) Week 3: readout and recommended config — model variant, quantization profile, autoscale settings, and cost projection.

Success criteria (proposed):
- Functional: inference output parity with your golden prompt set (we'll need a small labeled holdout).
- Performance: p95 < 350ms for requests <=800 tokens in baseline (no fallback). p99 < 1.2s for all requests.
- Reliability: <1% error rate across probes; graceful fallback to reduced-cost variant with <2x latency degradation.

Immediate asks / next steps (action owners):

Summary of what we heard (confirm or correct):
- Workload: clinical summarization + triage; mostly short requests, 5% long-tail (~2.4k tokens).
- Concurrency: 120 steady, burst up to 700 for 10–20s windows during batch jobs.
- Desired p95 latency: 350ms for requests <800 tokens.
- Security: SOC2 + VPC peering required for the POC.

Proposed POC probe plan (3-week technical POC, extendable):
1) Week 0 (this week): intake + sample payload validation — we confirm tokenization and representative QPS pattern.
2) Week 1: controlled load probes — run staged RPS ramp to characterize latency curve (p50/p95/p99) across short, medium, long token bins (<=400, 401–1200, 1201–2400). We will include KV cache warm/cold runs.
3) Week 2: burst/soak and integration — simulate business-hour concurrency plus night batch spikes; exercise fallback routing to smaller model if SLOs violated.
4) Week 3: readout and recommended config — model variant, quantization profile, autoscale settings, and cost projection.

Success criteria (proposed):
- Functional: inference output parity with your golden prompt set (we'll need a small labeled holdout).
- Performance: p95 < 350ms for requests <=800 tokens in baseline (no fallback). p99 < 1.2s for all requests.
- Reliability: <1% error rate across probes; graceful fallback to reduced-cost variant with <2x latency degradation.

Hi Mateo,

We wrapped up our capacity expansion in late January and I’m reconciling the invoices for Q4/Q1. Finance flagged a ~1.9M token delta for January (post-invoice) that wasn’t included in the committed-capacity true-up you and I discussed on the call.

Hi Samantha — thanks for the export and the heads-up. I pulled the usage summary on our side and see the same delta in the raw token logs after the capacity increase: there were a handful of burst windows where the committed pool usage exceeded the contracted threshold, and our system recorded them as billable overage.

Proposal: we can issue a one-time invoice for the delta (breakdown attached) and offer a 20% goodwill credit on the billable overage given this was the first month after expansion and we missed a short callout on burst protection. That brings the net one-time charge to $7,800 (net of credits). If Horizon prefers, we can instead apply the net amount as an adjustment on the March invoice to simplify remittance.

Next steps I suggest:
- You confirm preferred path (separate invoice vs March adjustment)
- If separate invoice, share PO or billing contact for remittance
- If adjust to March, confirm we have approval to proceed and we will reflect it on your 3/1 invoice.

— Mateo

mateo.alvarez@redwood.com

Summary:
- Raw delta: ~1.9M tokens (Jan 16-28 burst windows)
- Gross overage amount: $9,750 (before goodwill credit)
- Proposed goodwill credit (Mateo): 20% -> net $7,800

Considerations:
- If Horizon wants a separate invoice, revenue ops will need a PO number and the DocuSign if they require signed acceptance of the one-time charge.
- If we roll into March invoice, we should add a line item titled "Post-term usage adjustment (Jan burst windows)" and include the CSV summary and a note about credit applied. This keeps collections on the same cadence but may delay their close.

Hi Elena —

Thanks again for partnering with us on the LucidTech integration. Procurement is preparing the PO but we need to confirm a couple items on the insurance/COI so billing can move forward without delays. Can you attach the current COI and W9 (if available) and confirm the following: 

- Policy limits: is the general liability at least $1M per occurrence / $2M aggregate?
- Additional insured: can Redwood Inference be added as Additional Insured on the commercial general liability and on subcontractor ops if applicable? If so, please confirm the exact endorsement language.
- Waiver of subrogation: available? If not, please note.
- Any subcontractors who will have access to our environment (list names and their coverage).

Timeline note: our target for PO issuance is the week of March 1. If there are endorsement steps that require carrier processing, flag them now so we can issue a conditional PO if needed.

Redwood Inference

Hi Mateo —

Thanks for the checklist. I attached the current COI and our W9. Quick answers inline: 

- General liability: 1,000,000 each occurrence / 2,000,000 aggregate — confirmed on COI.
- Additional insured: We can add Redwood as Additional Insured on the GL for the integration work. Our broker provided the endorsement template; carrier needs 5–7 business days to issue the formal endorsement.
- Waiver of subrogation: Not currently included by default; we can request it but it may require underwriting approval.
- Subcontractors: We use Brightline Labs for specific edge deployment tasks — they carry a COI and I attached their declaration page as well.

We understand your PO timing. If Redwood needs a conditional PO to start work while the endorsement processes with the carrier, we can accept that, provided the final endorsement is in place within 30 days of PO date.

title: Follow-up: Delta Summit intro & next steps on inference evaluation
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Li <samantha.li@quantixa.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 14 Feb 2027 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Follow-up: Delta Summit intro & next steps on inference evaluation

Hi Samantha — great meeting you at Delta Summit today. Evan, good to meet you as well.

Quick recap from our 20-minute chat: we discussed Quantixa's plan to add live inference for product recommendation and the two things you called out as priorities: low 95th-percentile latency under 120ms and predictable per-request cost at scale. You mentioned the main blockers are integration effort and proving that a cheaper open-model + quantization approach can meet your quality bar.

Proposal / next steps I suggested:
- Pilot (2–4 week): Redwood hosted endpoint with a small dedicated pool, run your top-10 prompts + CTR reranker.
- Success criteria: P95 < 120ms at 99% availability; embedding similarity delta < 2% vs current baseline on the provided eval set.
- Data/inputs we need from you: 500 representative requests (anonymized), current latency histogram, and a short eval list (~200 examples) for quality checks.
